adult:
Your brain files the day while you sleep. Skip it too often and things stop sticking — meetings, names, where you left your keys.

Consistent sleep beats one heroic ten-hour catch-up.

Light, meals, and bedtime all tell your body what time it is. Send mixed signals and your mood and focus will let you know about it.

A dull wind-down routine is actually doing a lot of work.

Eating a big meal late at night tends to make blood-sugar control harder — even if the total calories are identical to an earlier meal.

It’s not about willpower. It’s just how the body prefers its schedule.

Ten minutes of walking, cycling, or anything that gets you moving tends to clear the mental fog fairly reliably. It’s one of the more reproducible findings in cognitive research.

You don’t need a full workout session. You just need to move.
Piggyback on what you already do

“After I make coffee, I do two minutes of stretching.” The existing habit does the reminding — you don’t have to remember anything new.

Small links are more durable than big resolutions.

“If it’s Tuesday at 7pm, I go to the gym” outperforms “I should exercise more” every time. Specificity removes the daily decision.

Writing the when and where is most of the work done.
Why checking a box feels good

Your brain pays attention to better-than-expected outcomes. A small visible win — a tick, a streak — registers as one. That’s not shallow; it’s just how learning works.

Aim that wiring at things worth repeating.

Some brains genuinely struggle to feel how long things take without external cues. Calendars, alarms, and timers aren’t crutches — they’re just the right tool for that particular wiring.

Vague plans fail specific people. Specificity is the fix.

Research is pretty consistent: self-criticism after a slip makes quitting more likely, not less. A calm restart keeps more people going long-term.

Flat tyre. Fix it. Drive on.

Writing about tangled thoughts for a few minutes consistently reduces rumination in study after study. You’re not trying to write well — you’re just clearing the inbox.

Messy notes count. Full sentences not required.

Regular readers carry larger vocabularies even when their formal education looks identical on paper. Fifteen quiet minutes a day compounds over years.

Speed-reading trophies optional.

Mindfulness practice produces modest but reliable gains on attention measures — not magic, just practice applied to a specific skill.

Short and daily beats long and occasional.

Spreading practice across days beats cramming it all into one session — for skills, facts, and habits alike. Your brain consolidates better with gaps in between.

Show up regularly. Let the gaps do half the work.
Who you think you are matters

People who say “I’m someone who exercises” stick with it longer than people who say “I’m trying to exercise more.” Identity outruns motivation.

The story you tell on good days is the voice you hear on hard ones.

Shoes by the door. Gym bag already packed. Water bottle already filled. Environment design consistently beats willpower when you’re tired.

Reduce friction. Future-you will be grateful.
Other people help you show up

Knowing someone is loosely watching — a friend, a partner, a group chat — reliably nudges follow-through. You don’t need a coach.

Sometimes one text buddy is enough.
When stress hits, old habits win

When stress hits, your brain reaches for whatever habit loop is already wired in — helpful or not.

That is why a simple, clear plan beats a clever one on hard days. Fewer choices, same good defaults.

The first thing poor sleep dulls is your ability to pause before reacting. Everything gets louder, smaller decisions feel bigger.

Protecting sleep is protecting your judgement — not a soft priority.

Short breaks from sitting improve energy ratings and help blood-sugar regulation after long desk stretches. The bar is genuinely low.

Stand up, walk to the kitchen, come back. That counts.

Real-world studies put habit formation at weeks to months, not the famous 21 days. Early repetitions are the investment. Ease is what you get later.

You’re not slow. You’re just early.

People who treat ability as buildable come back after setbacks more readily than those who see it as fixed. A bad week is noise, not a ceiling.

The only thing a slip proves is that you’re still in the game.

When you eat matters as well as what you eat.
In trials, earlier eating tends to suit metabolism better than late eating.

Strength training supports bones, metabolism, and your ability to carry your own shopping decades from now. Two sessions a week is a legitimate longevity habit.

Not about aesthetics. About function.

Every task switch leaves residual mental noise that slows down whatever comes next. Protecting a block of uninterrupted time isn’t a luxury — it’s how actual deep work gets done.

Batch the similar stuff. Guard the focused time.
A gap is not a verdict

Missing once doesn’t erase progress. Shame spirals do. The quickest recovery is the next small win — not a Monday restart ceremony.

Comebacks count as data too.

Evening light from phones delays melatonin — the signal that tells your body it’s time to wind down. Your brain can’t quite tell a screen from a sunny window.

Dimming helps. Distance helps more.

Mild dehydration — the kind where you don’t feel particularly thirsty — is enough to measurably dent concentration and mood. Most people walk around in it.

Full water bottle on the desk. Boring and effective.

People consistently generate more ideas while walking than sitting still. If you’re stuck, a lap around the block is not procrastination.

Move first. Judge the ideas when you’re back.

Large studies show people who cycle to work have meaningfully lower heart disease rates than those who drive the same route.

No lycra required.

Writing a goal down with a concrete plan consistently outperforms keeping it in your head. The act of writing it forces enough specificity to make it real.

Paper is cheap accountability.

Switch tasks before finishing a thought and part of your brain stays on the old one. That leftover hum makes the new task harder than it actually is.

Jot a quick note before you jump. Costs ten seconds.
Start so small it feels silly

“Two push-ups” beats “get fit” because it’s impossible to argue with. The first step should have no resistance at all.

Win first. Scale later.
Rest is part of the program

Adaptation happens during recovery, not during effort. Skipping rest doesn’t add progress — it just delays it and increases the chance something breaks.

Schedule it. Treat it like a set.

Children in households with predictable routines show calmer and healthier patterns across multiple measures of wellbeing. Not rigid — just consistent.

You’re building a felt sense of safety. That’s the whole point.

Just logging purchases — without any budget lecture attached — tends to reduce discretionary spending on its own. Awareness does most of the work.

The log is the intervention.

Self-control is not a fixed gift — it trains like anything else. Each time you follow through, the next start gets a little easier to reach.

You are not faking discipline; you are collecting receipts.

Sports science shows people often feel maxed out while their body still has a large reserve. The brain yells “stop” before the tank is empty.

On a brutal habit day, slow down — but question the story that you have nothing left.

Some nervous systems need louder outside cues — calendars, lists, apps — to feel time and reward fairly. That is biology, not laziness.

Use the scaffolding that lets you live the life you chose.

The same circuits that light up for a gold star can light up for a workout or a slot machine. Healthy systems borrow that wiring on purpose.

Direction matters more than the fact that chemistry notices.
Focus is a bundle of skills

Planning, starting, sticking, and switching are related but separate mental skills. They differ between people and can improve with practice.

Every steady week is quiet training for all of them.

When rewards arrive on an unpredictable rhythm, behaviour tends to stick harder than with a clockwork prize — that is old psychology lab wisdom.

Random insights here echo mild curiosity — separate from your weekly treats and pot rules, not an extra game layered on top.

Knowing someone else knows your goal nudges follow-through even if they never comment.

Pick an audience you trust; visibility is the tool.

Some people honestly mis-feel how long tasks take without clocks and reminders. That is not a character flaw — it is a mismatch between brain and vague plans.

Concrete times and places are the kind fix.

“If X happens, I will do Y” plans routinely beat “I will try harder” in study after study.

The sentence is the intervention.

Rooms, layouts, and visible cues predict doing the habit better than how motivated you felt that morning.

Design for Tuesday-you, not Sunday-night-you.

People often follow what they think peers do, sometimes more than their private values say.

Use that wisely: find a group where the normal thing is showing up.

A noticeable slice of adults meet criteria for attention-deficit conditions; many never get a label but still benefit from structure.

If a system helps you function, that is the outcome that counts.

Having several small dependable habits correlates with life satisfaction even when the habits themselves are humble.

Rhythm is its own kind of rest.

People restart goals more often after Mondays, month beginnings, or birthdays — “temporal landmarks” in the research.

A Wednesday miss is not a season finale; Thursday can be episode two.

Industry data suggests only a small share of people who download habit apps are still active two months later. By logging here you’re already on the uncommon side of that curve.

That’s the structure you built doing its job — not luck.

Plenty of animals wake on cue, but only humans set an alarm, understand what it means, and then debate whether nine more minutes is “strategic rest.”

The snooze button is a tiny comedy about self-control every morning.

Over a life, sleep adds up to decades — more than many mortgages last. Unlike a mortgage, good sleep pays you back in mood and focus.

Treat the mattress like infrastructure, not luxury.
The liver keeps its own clock

Roughly one standard drink per hour is what typical livers clear — coffee, cold showers, and pep talks do not speed the chemistry.

Patience is the only life hack here.
Eleven days awake (please do not try)

In 1964, a 17-year-old stayed awake for 11 days as a supervised science stunt — still the longest documented wakefulness on record. Doctors monitored him the whole time; he slept normally afterward and later said he would never do it again.

Sleep is not optional. Even a few short nights add up faster than you can power through.

Gyms spike in early January, then quiet down by February. March regulars rarely have more “motivation” — they have a plan that survived February.

Systems beat vibes.
The body moves before the story

Classic lab work found brain activity shifting before people reported a conscious choice to move. Much of life is noticing yourself mid-habit.

Automatic is not the enemy — it is the finish line.

Many people who make the bed say the day feels a little easier to start. Research is mixed on how much the bed itself matters — but a tiny win before breakfast can set a calm tone.

Two minutes, one clear done — small, but real.

Some surveys find phones checked nearly a hundred times a day — tiny hits of novelty all waking hours.

If your habit app feels sticky, you are competing with world-class designers. Fair fight.
A mile of jogging often burns fewer calories than a rich pastry contains. Annoying but true — before you eat a treat, it helps to translate its calories into the effort you would need to burn them.

“Deadline” once meant a line prisoners could not cross — serious consequences. Now it means spreadsheets.

Your Week still has a firm line here: logging for that Mon–Sun row locks at midnight Sunday (local time) — gentler stakes, same human love of a boundary.

A well-known study found judges’ leniency dipped before meal breaks and rose after — probably fatigue and timing, not cruelty.

Your 4pm self is not morally worse; just depleted.

The “three-week habit” story came from a surgeon’s guess decades ago. Real studies show weeks to many months depending on the habit and the person.

You are not behind — you are on a real curve.

Average humans walk enough miles in a life to circle Earth several times — mostly in small trips.

The “extra” walks for health are a rounding error that still changes risk.

Big studies link cycling to work with much lower heart-disease risk than driving the same route.

Lycra optional; pedals sufficient.

Muscles do not care if the weight has opinions. Carrying a toddler upstairs still counts as load.

Parenting: accidental gym.

Roman “average lifespan ~35” mostly reflects child deaths. Adults who survived childhood often reached their sixties and seventies.

Averages hide different stories.

Most cells replace on a multi-year rhythm — you are a slow ship of Theseus.

Today’s habits feed tomorrow’s replacement crew.

Putting things off often dodges boredom, anxiety, or shame — not a calendar glitch.

Name the feeling, shrink the task, start ugly.

Long-term stress nudges appetite toward quick calories — an old survival script.

Not weakness; wiring you can plan around.

That urge to squeeze a puppy? Researchers gave it a name: cute aggression. Harmless, common, weirdly sweet.

Totally unrelated to your streak — just a smile break.

Classic decision research found people dislike losing about twice as much as they enjoy gaining the same amount. A clear “floor” puts that to work: protecting progress motivates more than a vague “do better.”

Below the floor you’re in recovery: only core habits add to the pot, and a further drop resets it. This way you can focus on Core habits and to no further drops to protect your pot.

In real-world trials, savers who chose accounts that locked funds until a date often built bigger balances than people left totally free — even when freedom sounds nicer.

A named pot is a soft version of that contract with yourself.

Coffee-shop studies showed people speed up purchases as a free-drink card nears full — effort rises near the finish. A filling progress bar borrows the same nudge.

Close targets feel magnetic; use that instead of guilt.

Economists model a pattern many people feel: distant rewards shrink in our minds while tonight’s treat grows huge. Pairing a small weekly win with a slow pot feeds both versions of you.

Structure ends the argument instead of moralising it.

A giant review of health programmes found keeping track of behaviour — food, steps, sleep — was one of the strongest ingredients that made interventions work.

Your taps are not theatre; they are a proven behaviour-change tool.
Michie et al. — behaviour change techniques
Earn the fun, enjoy it harder

Researchers boosted gym visits by saving an addictive audiobook for treadmill time only. Your weekly treat works the same way: it arrives because you met your weekly commitment, so the fun and the habit stay linked.

A reward you earned tends to stick in memory longer than a reward you simply took.

Give someone a random mug and they suddenly price it like treasure. Streaks and pots inherit that quirk — losing ground stings because it feels like giving something up.

The app treats that feeling as leverage to strengthen your willpower even in difficult weeks.

Self-regulation theories say we compare “where I am” to “where I want to be,” then adjust. Colours, chips, and week rows are that comparison made friendly.

You get direction without drowning in shame.
Labels change spending in your head

People treat “holiday money” and “bill money” as if they are not interchangeable — even when the bank account is one. Separate goal pots ride that habit so wins stay attached to their story.

Compartmentalising can be a feature.

Brain cells learn from “happy/interesting surprises than expected” moments. In-app discovered insights like this one are a mild version of that — something to learn from while you stay mildly excited for the next one.

Small novelty can keep attention awake when it stays subtle.
The green pill around your name shows that you are currently online. When it's grey it means that you have lost connection, but your data will keep being saved on your device until next sync.
When you improve your consistency across weeks, it's reflected on your Consistency %. The colour change means keep doing what you are doing. Don't chase perfection, that is not a lasting change.

Early in a month, people often lean on fresh-start energy. Research on “temporal landmarks” shows Mondays and month beginnings nudge new attempts without needing a dramatic reboot.

If your goals sit in the steady band, you are allowed to ride that gentle tailwind.

Big studies on habits stress boring repetition over hero weeks. A new calendar year is just another Monday in a longer chain — useful if it helps you show up, not if it becomes all-or-nothing.

Steady beats dramatic when the year gets noisy.

Maintenance research keeps repeating one lesson: slips are normal; re-entry predicts long-term change more than a spotless streak.

Opening the log again after a rough week is already the behaviour scientists care about.
Habits added mid-week get a fair start

If you add a habit on a day other than Monday, that first partial week uses proportional targets — your threshold is scaled down based on how many days remain in the week.

For example, if your goal is 5 days per week and you start on Thursday, you only need to hit roughly 2 days (floor of 5 × 4/7). This keeps scoring fair without blocking your goal on a week you could never fully complete.

Core habits must all qualify for a closed week to count toward your % and weekly treats; growth habits add pot money when they qualify but never block the week. In recovery (rolling % below the 80% consistency floor), only core habits add £ to the pot; growth adds £0 until rolling % reaches the floor again.

Popular books like “The Molecule of More” and “Dopamine Nation” stress one idea: much of what people call “reward” is wanting and momentum, not the cozy feeling after a good meal.

Chasing the next tick is dopamine-heavy; deep rest after is often other chemistry.
Two styles of dopamine, one you

Lieberman and Long describe a “here-and-now” chemical chorus and a “future-and-possible” dopamine track that loves novelty and goals — both matter.

Balance is the game: enough future-pull to plan, enough present chemistry to enjoy arriving.
Lieberman & Long — The Molecule of More

Dopamine-related circuits learn from surprises and progress signals — “better than expected” keeps attention. That is why visible streaks and near-complete bars tug harder than abstract goals.

You are not weak; you are using a loud channel wisely.
Schultz et al.; Lieberman & Long (popular synthesis)
Pleasure and pain share a balance beam

Psychiatrist Anna Lembke argues endless easy hits — scrolls, snacks, buys — can tilt the brain toward needing more just to feel normal. Brief, chosen discomfort (effort, delay) resets the scale.

Habit apps that add friction on purpose echo that clinical lesson gently.
Oxytocin is a trust hormone, not only a cuddle hormone

Studies tie oxytocin to bonding cues: touch, warm voice, shared meals. It nudges social calm and “we” feelings.

Accountability partners and family habits may ride social chemistry, not only willpower.
Oxytocin has more than one mood

Researchers note context matters: the same hormone that supports closeness can sharpen feelings about “us versus them” in some settings.

Kindness with boundaries stays wise — chemistry is not a morality tale.
Serotonin is a stabiliser, not a smile pill

Most brain serotonin does not come with simple labels. It helps regulate mood, sleep timing, and even gut signalling — a steadying hand, not a happy switch.

Medication stories are separate from daily habits; both deserve nuance.

Exercise and outdoor light are linked to better mood and sleep in large studies — serotonin-related pathways are part of the story, alongside many other systems.

A walk is low-tech support for a complicated brain.

After intense pleasure, prolactin often rises — part of why excitement eases and the body shifts toward rest. It’s ordinary biology, not a failure of willpower or loss of interest.

Knowing about the cooldown helps you plan recovery instead of judging it.
Prolactin shows up for caregivers too

Hormone research links prolactin to nurturing behaviours in many mammals — milk production in some contexts and caregiving tone more broadly.

Bodies reward tending others with quiet chemistry.

Sapolsky’s “Behave” walks one behaviour through genes, hormones, context, and culture — dopamine, serotonin, oxytocin, and more act in concert like instruments, not soloists.

When you change a habit, you are conducting the whole band slowly.
Books beat doom-scrolling for the same circuits

Writers like Lembke and Lieberman describe how cheap digital hits train dopamine shortcuts. Swapping some scroll time for long-form explanations rebuilds patience — the same patience habits need.

This app’s slower rewards are a cousin of that swap.

Research on habit formation shows “automaticity” emerges over weeks to months, with wide individual differences — not a single fixed number for everyone.

Earning this tier means your logged behaviour met the bar over roughly a month of calendar days for this habit — celebrate showing up.

As windows lengthen, off-days age out of the lookback while you keep accumulating proof you can return.

This tier is about sustaining the pattern on the Log across roughly two months of calendar days.
Mastery III — you own this here

Meeting a high bar over the longest mastery window (calendar days) in this app is rare air — you have built real consistency with this habit here.

Consider banking it for now and revisiting in 6–12 months if you want to reinforce it again.
A long pause after mastery is normal life. Choosing to return and meet the maintenance bar shows the habit is still yours — a deliberate reunion, not a rescue mission.
Years later, the signal is not perfection. It is that you came back and let the weeks add up again — quiet proof that the identity stuck.
Half a decade is enough seasons to fail, wander, and restart. Earning this means the pattern survived real life — not a streak fantasy.
A decade marker is as much about values as behaviour: some practices deserve to stay loosely tethered across everything else that changes.

For a small child, time has two settings: this moment, and everything else — which feels like infinity. That’s not immaturity. It’s how the brain is wired before it learns to feel the distance between today and next week.

Habits are how you train that distance to feel real.
Jean Piaget — developmental psychology; Gabor Maté, Scattered Minds

The young brain defaults to magical thinking — want something hard enough and it arrives. Most adults still carry a version of this around goals.

The system you’re building here is the direct answer to that: small repeated actions, not intention.
Jean Piaget; Gabor Maté, Scattered Minds

No one is born understanding that today’s choices shape next week. That link — cause, then effect, across time — is something the brain constructs gradually. In some people that construction takes longer.

That’s not a deficit. It’s just the starting point.
Jean Piaget — cognitive development; Gabor Maté, Scattered Minds

Piaget found that children don’t have a full grasp of time until around age seven. Before that, duration and consequence are genuinely hard to feel — not a behaviour choice.

For some brains, external structure does what that internal clock hasn’t fully built yet.
Jean Piaget — preoperational stage; Gabor Maté, Scattered Minds
You can’t bend time by wanting to

Piaget observed that young children believe they can stop, speed up, or slow time down. That instinct doesn’t always fully leave. If deadlines feel like suggestions and schedules seem to shift around you — that’s the same wiring, older.

Structure isn’t a workaround. It’s the actual solution.
Jean Piaget — preoperational stage; Gabor Maté, Scattered Minds

When you sleep, your brain sorts the day — a bit like tidying your room.

If you miss sleep a lot, remembering gets harder. A usual bedtime beats one huge catch-up night.

Sunlight, meals, and bedtime tell your body what time it is. Mixed signals can make you feel off.

A calm wind-down routine is doing real work, even if it feels boring.

A very big meal late at night can make blood sugar trickier to handle — even with the same food earlier.

It is biology, not “being bad.”

About ten minutes of walking or moving often clears a fuzzy head.

You do not need a huge workout — wiggle, walk, dance.
Hook new habits to old ones

Try: “After I brush my teeth, I do two stretches.” The old habit reminds you.

Tiny links beat giant promises.

“Tuesday after school I ride my bike” beats “I should move more.”

Brains love a clear plan.

Your brain likes little wins — checkmarks, stars, streaks.

Use that power for habits that help you.

Some brains need clocks, timers, or lists to feel how long things take. That is okay — tools are helpers.

“Someday” is fuzzy; “after snack” is friendly.

Being mean to yourself after a slip makes it harder to try again.

Fix it like a flat bike tyre: patch it and ride on.

A few minutes of messy writing can untangle worried thoughts.

You do not need perfect sentences.

Reading a little most days grows vocabulary over time.

Short, cosy reading still counts.

Calm attention practice can train focus a bit like practice for sport.

Short and often beats rare and long.

A bit on many days beats one giant cram for skills and habits.

Gaps between tries help your brain learn.

Saying “I am a reader” or “I move my body” can stick better than “I am trying.”

Tell a story that helps you on hard days.

Lay out clothes, fill a water bottle, put shoes by the door — easy setups beat arguing with a tired brain.

Less friction, more follow-through.

When someone kind knows your plan, you are more likely to do it.

A friend, parent, or coach counts.

When stressed, brains reach for familiar routines fast — good or not.

Keep your plan simple before stress arrives.

Poor sleep makes it harder to pause before you snap or give up.

Sleep protects your choices.

Short breaks from sitting help energy and sugar control.

Stand, stretch, grab water — it counts.

Real life habits often need weeks or months, not magic “21 days.”

Early tries are not failure — they are the start.

People who believe skills can grow bounce back after a bad day.

A rough week is a bump, not the end.

More food earlier in the day often suits the body better than only huge late meals.

Timing is a tool, not a rule about “good” or “bad.”

Strength helps bones and everyday jobs like carrying bags.

Two short sessions a week is a big win.

Switching tasks leaves mental “echoes” that slow you down.

Give one job a protected block of time.
A miss is not the end

Skipping once does not erase progress. Shame spirals do more damage.

The next small win is the comeback.

Bright screens at night can delay sleepy chemistry.

Dim screens or move them farther away before bed.

Even mild thirst can ding mood and attention.

Keep water handy — boring and useful.

Walking often brings more ideas than sitting.

Stuck? Try a lap before deciding.

Big studies link biking with healthier hearts than only driving.

Pedals do not need fancy clothes.

Goals with a written plan beat goals only in your head.

Paper or phone notes both work.
Finish a thought before you jump

If you switch tasks mid-thought, part of your brain stays behind.

Jot a note in ten seconds, then switch.

“Two jumps” beats “get fit forever” for actually starting.

Make step one too easy to argue with.

Muscles and skills grow during recovery, not only during effort.

Rest is part of training.

Predictable rhythms help kids feel safe and healthy — not rigid, just steady.

You are building a calm home feeling.

Just noticing what you buy often lowers extra spending — no lecture needed.

The list is the magic.

Following through trains “stop and go” skills like sport practice.

Each small keep-trying counts.

Bodies often have more gas than the brain admits on hard days.

Slow down, but question “I have nothing left.”

Some brains need louder outside reminders. That is wiring, not laziness.

Use what works for you.

Stars, ticks, and games use similar “win” wiring — healthy apps borrow that on purpose.

Steer the wins toward good habits.

Planning, starting, sticking, and switching are different skills.

Steady weeks quietly train all of them.

Random little rewards can make habits stickier than perfect clocks.

This app’s surprise facts are gentle curiosity — separate from weekly treats and jar rules, not an extra game on top.

When a trusted person knows your goal, you are more likely to try.

They do not have to nag.

Some people mis-guess how long tasks take without a clock. That is human, not “lazy.”

Concrete times fix it kindly.

“If it rains, I dance inside” beats “I will try harder.”

The sentence is the superpower.

Visible cues and layouts predict habits better than morning excitement.

Design for Tuesday-you.

People often copy what friends seem to do.

Pick friends or groups where showing up is normal.

Many people have attention differences; structure still helps everyone.

If a tool helps you live your plan, it wins.

Several small steady habits link to happier days — even humble ones.

Rhythm can feel like rest.

Mondays, month starts, and birthdays nudge new tries.

A Wednesday miss is not the finale.

Most habit apps lose people in a couple of months. Logging here already puts you on the uncommon side.

Credit your effort.

Only humans set alarms… then negotiate nine more minutes.

The snooze button is a tiny comedy about mornings.

Sleep adds up to a huge slice of life — like a long holiday every year.

Good sleep pays you back in mood.

Typical grown-up bodies clear about one standard drink per hour — coffee and pep talks do not speed it up.

Waiting is the real “life hack.”

He slept normally after and said never again. Your sleep matters every night.

Now it means rows in an app — gentler stakes.

Goal pots here echo that gentle lock.

People speed up when a punch-card is almost full.

Progress bars use the same “almost there” pull.

Weekly treats plus a slow pot feed both versions of you.

Your weekly treat after qualifying uses the same bargain.

The app respects feelings, not only numbers.

Surprise facts here are mild curiosity — little brain teasers, not bets.
Green around your name means you are online! Grey means you are not!

Jumping from struggling to solid usually means several good closed weeks — not one fluke week.

Let the colour change say “keep going,” not “be perfect.”

Month starts give a fresh-start boost for many people.

If you are in the steady band, ride the gentle tailwind.

Habits love boring repetition more than hero weeks.

A new year is useful if it helps you show up — not if it becomes all-or-nothing.

Slips are normal. Scientists care most that you return.

Opening the log after a rough week already counts.

If you add a habit mid-week, your first partial week uses a smaller target so it stays fair.

Example: starting Thursday with a 5-day goal might ask ~2 days that week.

Core habits are your must-do habits: when a week closes, they all need to qualify so the week counts on your score and for your treat.

Growth habits add jar money when you do them — they never block the week.

In recovery (below your floor), only core adds jar money until your score climbs back; growth waits. A grown-up can help with the numbers.

A lot of what people call “reward” is wanting and go-go energy, not only cosy afterglow.

Chasing the next tick uses different chemistry than deep rest.

Writers describe “here-and-now” chemistry and “future-and-maybe” chemistry — both matter.

Balance planning with enjoying the moment.

Brains learn from surprises and progress signals.

Visible streaks use a loud, honest channel — not a weakness.

Endless quick hits — scrolls, snacks — can tilt the brain toward needing more to feel normal.

Chosen effort and breaks can reset the scale.

Oxytocin links to bonding cues: touch, warm voices, shared meals.

Family habits ride social chemistry, not only willpower.

The same hormone can nudge closeness or “us vs them” feelings depending on context.

Kindness plus boundaries stays wise.

Serotonin helps mood, sleep timing, and even gut signals — a stabiliser, not a simple “happy switch.”

Medicine stories are separate from daily habits.

Exercise and outdoor light link to better mood and sleep in big studies.

A walk is simple support for a complicated brain.

After very intense fun, the body shifts toward calmer chemical state — that is normal biology.

Understanding cooldown helps you plan rest, not worry.

Hormone research ties prolactin to nurturing behaviours in many mammals.

Bodies quietly reward caring for others.

One behaviour involves genes, hormones, context, and culture together — like a band, not one instrument.

Changing a habit slowly conducts the whole band.

Cheap digital hits train shortcuts; longer reading rebuilds patience.

This app’s slower rewards echo that gentler pace.

adult: The app that helps you discover, build, and become your better self.
Get ready to change your life one week at a time. Here you’ll grow the consistency you always needed but couldn’t quite stick with.
You can edit your name and add your birth date. If your age is 0–8 you’ll have a different experience. Tracking will start from today.

kid: The app that helps you discover, build, and become your better self.
Get ready to grow good habits one week at a time — small steps that add up.
You can edit your name and add your birthday. If you are 0–8 things look a bit friendlier. Tracking starts from today — a grown-up can help with dates.

adult: A few things worth knowing before you start.

This app helps you build consistency for everything you struggle with until now, using three mechanisms:

  • Goal — long-term rewards so you keep your eyes on what you are saving for.
  • Weekly treat — short-term rewards so you feel the win often.
  • Consistency — a streak you will want to keep going.
Consistency is built on mundane repetition. But this app makes it interesting and fun. Life is neither easy nor perfect, but we think it’s OK to be on top of things at least 80% of the time.
Qualify your week to remain Consistent for 80% of your tracking time and you can reap your big Goal reward, on top of building the good habits you really need.

A week qualifies by qualifying its (core) habits. More on this later.

You are now ready to set your first Goal.

kid: A few things worth knowing before you start.

This app uses three ideas:

  • A big Goal to save for.
  • A small weekly Treat when you do well.
  • A Consistency streak you want to protect.

Ask a grown-up if a sentence feels tricky.
Consistency means showing up again and again. This app tries to make that feel fair and a bit fun. Aim to be on track most weeks — grown-ups often use about 80% as a guide.
When your week qualifies, you stay Consistent over your tracked weeks and move toward your big Goal while building habits.

A week qualifies when its core habits qualify. Grown-ups can read more in Settings → Help.

Next: add your first Goal.

adult: Name something you’re working toward — often something you could save toward over weeks or months. Think inspiring target, not a vague chore.

kid: Pick something big you want — like saving for something special. Make it something that makes you feel excited.

adult: This is a small reward for a good week — like a film night or a favourite snack. It’s separate from your big goal; it isn’t for weight-style outcomes.

kid: Pick a little treat for a good week — like a fun outing or a small thing you like. Keep it small and fun, not big scary goals.

adult: Use small repeatable actions — like “walk 20 minutes” or “read before bed”. Avoid vague outcomes (“lose 30kg”), and don’t use your weekly treat as a habit.

kid: Write a small thing you do again and again — like “read 10 minutes”. Big goals and treats go in the other boxes.

adult:
Core — Every core habit must qualify in a week for that week to count as on track.

Growth — Both core and growth habits add money to your pots at the end of the week when they qualify.

Recovery mode — When your consistency % drops below the 80% floor, only core habits add to your pot until you recover (more on this later).

kid: Pick your Tier: core or growth

Core habits are the big “must-do” ones for a goal. After a week closes, every core habit for that goal needs to qualify so that week counts on your score circle and for your weekly treat.

Growth habits are extra helpful habits. When they qualify, they add jar money — but missing them never blocks the week from counting.

Recovery means your rolling score dropped below the 80% floor. Then only core habits add jar money until your score climbs back — growth habits wait.

Ask a grown-up to open Tips in Settings if you want the longer friendly guide.
